title: Communicating with Someone Living with Dementia
summary: How you talk matters as much as what you say. A few consistent habits make daily conversations easier for everyone.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Communicating with Someone Living with Dementia | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: How you talk matters as much as what you say. A few consistent habits make daily conversations easier for everyone.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Correcting factual mistakes rarely helps and often causes distress.
Short, simple sentences work better than long explanations.
Entering their reality is usually kinder than insisting on yours.

paragraphs: When someone with dementia says something factually wrong — believing a deceased spouse is alive, for instance — correcting them head-on often causes real distress without changing the underlying confusion. Gently redirecting the conversation, or responding to the emotion behind the statement rather than the fact itself, tends to work better.

Simple, one-idea-at-a-time sentences are easier to follow than multi-part questions. "Would you like tea?" lands better than "Do you want tea or coffee, and should I bring it in the blue mug or the other one?"

nextSteps: Practice redirecting rather than correcting, even in small daily moments.
Reduce background noise during conversations — it makes a bigger difference than expected.
Share these approaches with other family members so the response stays consistent.
disclaimer: General communication guidance — a speech-language pathologist or dementia care specialist can offer strategies tailored to a specific stage or situation.
